summary:
Transposition of great arteries with an intact ventricular septum generally lacks ductus-dependent circulation due to absent pressure gradients that drive ductal flow, whereas most congenital heart diseases involving obstruction or low output depend heavily on a patent ductus arteriosus for maintaining perfusion through compensatory mechanisms such as right-to-left shunts or bypass functions. This makes option A distinct from those requiring ductal support for hemodynamic stability.

Okay, I need to summarize Agent2’s reasoning based strictly on its core clinical insights related to ductus-dependent blood flow—without adding external details beyond what was explicitly stated.

Final summary:

Transposition of great arteries with an intact ventricular septum rarely relies on ductus-dependent flow due to insufficient pressure gradients driving shunt formation, while most other specified congenital defects depend significantly on a patent ductus arteriosus for circulatory maintenance via compensatory pathways. Hence, this condition differs fundamentally in terms of physiological necessity for ductal patency.
